content.summary: Apple scab is the most common disease of apple and crabapple trees in Minnesota, caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. It infects both leaves and fruit, rendering fruit unfit for eating and causing early leaf drop that weakens the tree over time. Planting disease-resistant varieties is the best management strategy.
content.full_text: Apple scab of apples and crabapples. Apple scab is caused by the fungus Venturia inaequalis. It infects crabapples and apples (Malus spp.), mountain ash (Sorbus spp.), pear (Pyrus communis) and Cotoneaster (Cotoneaster spp.). Identification: Leaf spots are round, olive-green, up to 1/2 inch across, velvet-like with fringed borders. Spots turn dark brown to black and grow together with age. Infected fruit have olive-green spots that turn brown and corky. Fruit infected when young become deformed and cracked. Lifecycle: The fungus overwinters on fallen diseased leaves. In spring, spores are released and carried by wind to new growth. Spores need several hours of moisture to start new infections. Secondary infections occur every 9-17 days. Warm, rainy spring and summer conditions are ideal. Management: Clean up leaves in fall, prune for open canopy, plant resistant varieties. Fungicides are preventative and must be timed from half-inch green tip through petal fall. Fungicide options for edible fruit include captan, lime-sulfur and sulfur. For ornamental crabapples: tebuconazole, myclobutanil, captan, chlorothalonil, propiconazole, mancozeb, sulfur/lime sulfur, neem oil, copper.

sections.heading: Quick facts
sections.text: Apple scab is the most common disease of apple and crabapple trees in Minnesota. Caused by a fungus infecting both leaves and fruit. Scabby fruit are often unfit for eating. Infected leaves have olive-green to brown spots. Leaf loss weakens the tree when it occurs many years in a row. Planting disease-resistant varieties is the best management approach. Fungicides require proper timing to be effective.

sections.heading: How to identify apple scab
sections.text: Leaf spots are round, olive-green, up to 1/2 inch across, velvet-like with fringed borders. As they age, spots turn dark brown to black, enlarge and merge. Spots often form along leaf veins. Heavily spotted leaves turn yellow and drop by mid-summer. Infected fruit develop olive-green spots that turn brown and corky. Fruit infected when very young become deformed and cracked as they grow.

sections.heading: Life cycle of apple scab
sections.text: The apple scab fungus has host-specific strains. The fungus overwinters on fallen diseased leaves. In spring, spores are shot into the air and carried by wind to new leaves, flowers, fruit or green twigs. Spores need several hours of moisture on the plant surface to start new infections. Infections grow into spots that produce more spores within 9 to 17 days. The infection cycle repeats throughout the growing season whenever leaves remain wet long enough. Warm, rainy spring and summer weather creates ideal conditions. Several years of early leaf loss can cause decreased growth, reduced bloom and increased susceptibility to winter injury.

sections.heading: How to manage apple scab - Cultural practices
sections.text: Clean up leaves in fall: rake up and destroy fallen leaves before the first snowfall. Infected leaves can be burned, buried or composted, or chopped with a mulching lawn mower. Plant and prune correctly: do not overcrowd plants. Prune trees so branches are spaced to allow air movement and quick leaf drying. Remove upright suckers and water sprouts from the main trunk or canopy.

sections.heading: Apple and crabapple varieties for Minnesota
sections.text: Resistant to apple scab: Honeycrisp. Immune to apple scab: Dayton, Freedom, Liberty, McShay, Pixie Crunch, Pristine, Redfree, William's Pride. Likely to be infected: Beacon, Fireside, Haralson, Keepsake, Paul Red, Sweet Sixteen, Wealthy, Chestnut Crab. Very likely to be infected: Cortland, Honeygold, McIntosh, State Fair, Zestar!

sections.heading: Using fungicides - Ornamental crabapples
sections.text: Fungicides protect healthy leaves but do not cure existing infections. Sprays must start at the half-inch green tip stage in spring and be repeated until most flower petals have fallen. Spray interval should be shortened during frequent rain or where scab has been severe. Two well-timed spring fungicide applications often suffice for ornamentals. Do not apply fungicides to already-diseased trees. Active ingredients for ornamentals only: tebuconazole, myclobutanil, captan, chlorothalonil, propiconazole, mancozeb, sulfur/lime sulfur, neem oil, copper.

sections.heading: Using fungicides - Edible apples and crabapples
sections.text: Start sprays at the half-inch green tip stage. Adjust spray interval based on rainfall and disease history. In mid-June, examine leaves thoroughly; stop spraying if no or very few scab lesions are found. If scab is found or infected trees are nearby, continue spraying. Fungicide options for edible fruit: captan, lime-sulfur, powdered or wettable sulfur. Do not use all-purpose fruit sprays except just after petal fall, and never during bloom as they kill pollinators.

content.summary: Black rot is an occasional fungal disease of Minnesota apple trees caused by Diplodia seriata (syn. Botryosphaeria obtusa). It causes leaf spot, fruit rot and branch cankers. Trees stressed by cold injury, drought, waterlogged soils or fire blight are most susceptible. Management focuses on good sanitation, proper tree care and pruning. Fungicides are rarely needed.
content.full_text: Black rot of apple. Black rot is occasionally a problem on Minnesota apple trees. This fungal disease causes leaf spot, fruit rot and cankers on branches. Trees are more likely to be infected if they are not fully hardy in Minnesota, infected with fire blight, or stressed by environmental factors like drought. Manage black rot by practicing good sanitation, taking good care of your trees and pruning correctly. Fungicides are rarely needed. Fruit symptoms: Large brown rotten areas most common on the blossom end, brown to black concentric rings, firm brown flesh, small black pycnidia on older infections, some fruit mummify on the tree. Leaf symptoms: Frog-eye leaf spot with purplish or reddish edges and light tan interiors. Branch symptoms: Sunken reddish-brown cankers with rough or cracked bark. Survival and spread: Fungus survives in branch cankers and mummified fruit. Spores released in wet weather and spread by wind or splashing water. Management: Prune out dead or diseased branches, remove mummified fruit, remove all infected plant material. Prune when temperatures are below 32°F. Maintain tree health by reducing stress. Fungicides captan and sulfur are rarely needed but can help prevent frog-eye leaf spot and fruit infection.

sections.heading: Quick facts
sections.text: Black rot is occasionally a problem on Minnesota apple trees. This fungal disease causes leaf spot, fruit rot and cankers on branches. Trees are more likely to be infected if they are not fully hardy in Minnesota, infected with fire blight, or stressed by environmental factors like drought. Manage black rot by practicing good sanitation, taking good care of trees and pruning correctly. Fungicides are rarely needed.

sections.heading: How to identify black rot - Fruit symptoms
sections.text: Large brown rotten areas can form anywhere on the fruit but are most common on the blossom end. Brown to black concentric rings are often visible on larger infections. The flesh of the apple is brown but remains firm. Small black spots called pycnidia (fungal spore-producing structures) can be seen on older fruit infections. Some fruit mummify and remain attached to the tree. Occasionally fruit infected early in the season ripen early and are rotten at the core.

sections.heading: How to identify black rot - Leaf symptoms
sections.text: Infected leaves develop frog-eye leaf spot: circular spots with purplish or reddish edges and light tan interiors.

sections.heading: How to identify black rot - Branch symptoms
sections.text: Cankers appear as sunken, reddish-brown areas on infected branches with rough or cracked bark. Cankers may be hard to see and often appear on the southwest side of young trees where winter injury is common, or at branch crotches that are last to harden off in fall.

sections.heading: How does black rot survive and spread?
sections.text: Black rot is caused by the fungus Diplodia seriata (syn. Botryosphaeria obtusa). The fungus survives Minnesota winters in branch cankers and mummified fruit attached to the tree. In wet weather, spores are released and spread by wind or splashing water. Leaf spots do not release fungal spores. The fungus infects leaves and fruit through natural openings or minor wounds, and branches and trunk through dead or damaged wood.

sections.heading: How does tree stress affect black rot?
sections.text: Minnesota's severe cold often injures apple trees, making cold-damaged tissue susceptible to infection. Trees damaged by drought stress or waterlogged soils are more likely to be affected — including trees on sandy soils without irrigation, trees not irrigated during dry spells, and trees in poorly drained soils. Trees infected by fire blight often later develop black rot, as fire blight results in dead wood easily infected by the black rot fungus.

sections.heading: How to manage black rot - Sanitation
sections.text: Prune out dead or diseased branches. Pick all dried and shriveled fruits remaining on the trees. Remove infected plant material from the area. All infected plant parts should be burned, buried or sent to a municipal composting site. Remove stumps of any apple trees cut down, as dead stumps can be a source of spores.

sections.heading: How to manage black rot - Pruning
sections.text: Prune trees when temperatures are below 32°F. February and March are good months to prune in Minnesota, as the fungus is not active until spring. First prune out all dead branches, then prune to shape the tree. Winter cuts will heal by spring and be less likely to become infected.

sections.heading: How to manage black rot - Tree care
sections.text: Choose an appropriate site for all new apple trees. Plant only cultivars that are truly hardy for your area. Provide trees with adequate water. Keep fire blight in check. Remove any limbs or trees killed by fire blight to discourage black rot.

sections.heading: How to manage black rot - Fungicide sprays
sections.text: Fungicide sprays are usually not needed for managing black rot in Minnesota. Consider using fungicides only after good cultural practices have been applied and the disease has not resolved. Captan and sulfur products labeled for both scab and black rot may help prevent frog-eye leaf spot and fruit infection but will not control or prevent branch infection.

fungicides.notes: Fungicides rarely needed. Effective against frog-eye leaf spot and fruit infection only; do not control branch infections.

content.summary: Cedar-apple rust is a fungal disease caused by Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae that requires two different host plants to complete its life cycle: junipers (including eastern red cedar) and apples or crabapples. It causes bright orange leaf spots and fruit damage on apples, and gall formation on juniper branches. Removing nearby junipers and planting resistant varieties are the most effective management strategies.
content.full_text: Cedar-apple rust is caused by the fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae. The disease requires two hosts to complete its life cycle: junipers such as eastern red cedar and plants in the rose family including apples and crabapples. On apple and crabapple trees, the disease appears as yellow spots on leaves that later turn bright orange with red borders. Small black dots may develop in the center of these spots. Later in the season, finger-like fungal tubes form on the underside of leaves and release spores that infect junipers. Fruit may develop irregular green to brown spots and black fungal structures but the infection is usually shallow. On junipers, the fungus forms round woody galls on branches. During rainy spring weather, these galls produce orange gelatinous tendrils that release spores. Spores travel by wind and infect nearby apple trees. Management includes removing nearby junipers, pruning infected branches, and planting resistant apple varieties.

sections.heading: Quick facts
sections.text: Cedar-apple rust is a fungal disease affecting apple and crabapple trees. The fungus requires both juniper and apple hosts to complete its life cycle. Bright orange leaf spots are the most common symptom on apples. The disease rarely kills trees but can reduce fruit quality and vigor.

sections.heading: How to identify cedar-apple rust
sections.text: Yellow spots appear on apple leaves in spring and later become bright orange or orange-red. Raised black dots may appear in the center of spots. Later, finger-like fungal structures grow from the underside of leaves. Fruit may develop irregular spots and minor deformation. On junipers, round woody galls form on branches and produce orange gelatinous tendrils during wet spring weather.

sections.heading: Life cycle of cedar-apple rust
sections.text: The fungus overwinters in galls on juniper branches. In spring, rain causes the galls to produce orange spore masses that release spores into the air. Wind carries spores to apple and crabapple leaves where infection occurs. Later in the season, spores produced on apple leaves infect nearby juniper plants. The fungus requires both hosts to complete its life cycle.

sections.heading: How to manage cedar-apple rust
sections.text: Management focuses on preventing infection and reducing sources of spores. Remove nearby juniper plants when possible, especially eastern red cedar. Prune and destroy galls on juniper branches before spring rains. Plant apple varieties resistant to cedar-apple rust. Fungicides are rarely necessary for home landscapes but may be used preventively in orchards or ornamental plantings.

sections.heading: Apple varieties for Minnesota
sections.text: Some apple varieties show resistance to cedar-apple rust. Resistant varieties help reduce disease severity and the need for chemical control.

content.summary: Cherry powdery mildew is caused by the fungus Podosphaera clandestina, affecting mid- and late-season sweet and sour cherries. It renders fruit unmarketable due to white fungal growth. Season-long control of leaves and fruit is critical to minimize disease pressure and protect developing fruit.
content.full_text: Cherry Powdery Mildew. Written by Claudia Probst and Gary Grove, WSU Plant Pathology. Powdery mildew of sweet and sour cherry is caused by Podosphaera clandestina, an obligate biotrophic fungus. Mid- and late-season sweet cherry (Prunus avium) cultivars are commonly affected, rendering them unmarketable due to white fungal growth on the cherry surface. Season long disease control of both leaves and fruit is critical to minimize overall disease pressure and protect developing fruit. Identification: Initial symptoms occur 7-10 days after first irrigation as light, roughly-circular powdery patches on young susceptible leaves. Older leaves develop ontogenic resistance. The disease initiates on undersides of leaves and spreads by wind. Early fruit infections begin in the cherry bowl area. Lifecycle: P. clandestina produces chasmothecia in fall that overwinter and release ascospores in spring during wetness events, causing primary infections. Secondary infections spread by wind. Conditions favoring disease: High humidity and temperatures of 70-80°F. Infection requires 1/10 inch of rain or irrigation and temperatures of 50°F or more. Cultural controls include irrigation management, pruning for airflow, and root sucker removal. Chemical controls: Most synthetic fungicides are preventative. Maintain consistent program from shuck fall through harvest. Rotate between modes of action to avoid resistance. Resistance management: Limit applications of single mode of action. Apply high risk compounds to no more than 30% of total sprays per season.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Cherry powdery mildew is caused by Podosphaera clandestina, an obligate biotrophic fungus affecting mid- and late-season sweet and sour cherry cultivars. Infection results in white fungal growth on the cherry surface, rendering fruit unmarketable. Season-long disease control of both leaves and fruit is critical.

sections.heading: Identification
sections.text: Initial symptoms appear 7-10 days after first irrigation as light, roughly-circular powdery patches on young susceptible leaves. Older leaves have ontogenic resistance. The disease begins on leaf undersides and can cause distortion and curling as it spreads by wind. Early fruit infections start in the cherry bowl area and require a hand lens to detect.

sections.heading: Lifecycle clues to management
sections.text: In fall, P. clandestina produces chasmothecia (overwintering structures) on senescent leaves or tree crotches. In spring, during wetness events, chasmothecia release ascospores causing primary infections. Spores disseminate by wind causing secondary infections. The fungus re-enters overwintering phase in early fall.

sections.heading: Conditions initiating and favoring disease
sections.text: Spring primary infections require 1/10 inch of rain or irrigation and temperatures of 50°F or more. The fungus does not need free water to thrive; high humidity and temperatures of 70-80°F favor the disease. Spore dispersal peaks late morning to early afternoon. Warm temperatures with morning fog or dew are ideal for rapid disease increase.

sections.heading: Cultural controls
sections.text: Manage irrigation: a two-week delay in irrigation can delay disease initiation with no negative impact on fruit. Pruning promotes airflow and reduces humidity favorable for disease. Remove root suckers as they harbor highly susceptible leaf tissue close to irrigation and humidity sources.

sections.heading: Chemical controls
sections.text: Most synthetic fungicides are preventative, not eradicative. Maintain a consistent program from shuck fall through harvest. Consider post-harvest preventative measures such as sulfur application. Rotate between modes of action (MOA) to avoid resistance. Preharvest fungicide applications should be based on residual period of the product.

sections.heading: Resistance management
sections.text: The FRAC code represents the mode of action of the fungicide. Group 11 fungicides are high risk for resistance development. Limit applications of a single mode of action both during the season and in sequence. Apply medium risk compounds no more than three times per season. High risk compounds should always be alternated with other modes of action and used in no more than 30% of total sprays per season.

authors: Tianna Dupont, WSU NCW Regional Specialist

content.summary: Gray leaf spot is typically the most serious foliar disease of corn in the U.S. corn belt, caused by the fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis. It requires extended periods of high humidity and warm conditions, and is a particular problem in minimum tillage and corn-on-corn rotation systems. Management includes resistant hybrids, conventional tillage, crop rotation, and foliar fungicides when economically warranted.
content.full_text: Gray leaf spot on corn. Gray leaf spot is typically the most serious foliar disease of corn in the U.S. corn belt. The disease first appears as small necrotic spots with halos, expanding to rectangular lesions about 1/8 inch wide by up to 2-3 inches long, gray to brown in appearance with distinct parallel edges. Mature lesions appear opaque when held up to light. Conditions favoring disease: moderate to warm temperatures during extended periods (greater than 24 hours) of high humidity (greater than 95 percent) or wet weather. A problem in areas with minimum tillage and corn-on-corn rotations. In susceptible hybrids, disease typically develops from silking to maturity. Causal pathogen: Cercospora zeae-maydis, a fungus whose only known host is corn, overwinters in debris on the soil surface. Disease management: resistant corn hybrids, conventional tillage where appropriate, crop rotation. Foliar fungicides can be effective if economically warranted, typically only profitable on susceptible inbreds or hybrids under high risk conditions with high yield potential, prolonged humid conditions, and evidence of disease development.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.text: The disease first appears as small necrotic spots with halos. These usually expand to become rectangular lesions about 1/8 inch wide by up to 2 to 3 inches long, gray to brown in appearance. Mature lesions usually have distinct parallel edges and appear opaque when held up to the light. Lesion shape and color varies widely by hybrid. Symptoms can sometimes be confused with northern corn leaf spot, although gray leaf spot lesions are usually limited on the sides by veins.

sections.heading: Conditions and timing that favor disease
sections.text: Conditions that favor infection include moderate to warm temperatures during extended periods (greater than 24 hours) of high humidity (greater than 95 percent) or wet weather. Gray leaf spot is a problem in areas with minimum tillage and corn-on-corn rotations. In susceptible hybrids, the disease typically develops from silking to maturity.

sections.heading: Causal pathogen
sections.text: The pathogen is a fungus called Cercospora zeae-maydis. Its only known host is corn and it overwinters in debris on the soil surface.

sections.heading: Disease management
sections.text: Disease management tactics include using resistant corn hybrids, conventional tillage where appropriate, and crop rotation. Foliar fungicides can be effective if economically warranted. They are typically only profitable on susceptible inbreds or susceptible hybrids under a combination of high risk conditions with high yield potential, prolonged humid conditions, and evidence of disease development.

key_phrases: most serious foliar disease corn belt

chemical.notes: Only economically warranted on susceptible inbreds or hybrids under high risk conditions with high yield potential, prolonged humid conditions, and evidence of disease development

content.summary: Common rust of corn is a fungal disease caused by Puccinia sorghi that produces reddish-brown rust pustules on corn leaves. The disease commonly appears in the northern United States during mid to late summer when spores are carried north by wind. It develops best in cool, moist weather but usually causes little yield loss in modern corn hybrids.
content.full_text: Common rust of corn is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. The disease produces rust-colored to dark brown pustules on both upper and lower surfaces of corn leaves. These pustules contain cinnamon-colored spores that can spread to other leaves and plants. As the disease progresses, pustules may darken and leaf tissue may turn yellow or die. The fungus does not typically overwinter in northern regions; instead, spores are blown northward from southern areas such as Mexico and the southern United States each summer. Young leaves are more susceptible to infection, especially during periods of cool temperatures, heavy dew, and high humidity. Disease development is favored by temperatures between about 60 and 76°F and several hours of leaf wetness. Management primarily relies on planting resistant hybrids and monitoring disease levels. Fungicide applications are rarely necessary in most commercial hybrid corn fields unless infections occur early and conditions remain favorable for disease spread.

sections.heading: Quick facts
sections.text: Common rust is a fungal disease that affects corn leaves. It produces reddish-brown pustules on both sides of leaves. The disease often appears in northern regions during mid to late summer. Yield loss is usually low in resistant hybrid corn.

sections.heading: How to identify common rust on corn
sections.text: Small rust-colored pustules form on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves. The pustules contain powdery spores and are typically elongated or oval. As they age, they turn darker brown or black. Severe infections can cause yellowing or death of leaf tissue.

sections.heading: Conditions that favor disease
sections.text: Common rust develops best under cool temperatures between about 60°F and 76°F combined with high humidity and prolonged leaf wetness. Heavy dew and wet weather promote infection. Temperatures above 80°F slow disease development.

sections.heading: Disease cycle
sections.text: The fungus does not survive winter in northern regions. Spores are carried by wind from southern areas into corn fields during early summer. Once pustules develop on leaves, new spores are produced and spread to other plants during the growing season.

sections.heading: Management
sections.text: Planting resistant corn hybrids is the most effective management strategy. Monitoring fields for rust development helps determine if control measures are needed. Fungicide applications may be used in severe cases but are rarely necessary for commercial hybrid corn.

content.summary: Black rot is one of the most serious diseases of grapes in the eastern United States, caused by the fungus Guignardia bidwelli. Crop losses can range from 5 to 80 percent depending on disease levels, weather, and variety susceptibility. The fungus infects all green parts of the vine, with fruit infection being most damaging. Management combines sanitation, resistant varieties, and timely fungicide applications from early shoot growth through 4-6 weeks after bloom.
content.full_text: Black Rot on Grapes in Home Gardens. Black rot is one of the most serious diseases of grapes in the eastern United States. Crop losses can range from 5 to 80 percent depending on the amount of disease in the vineyard, weather, and variety susceptibility. The fungus Guignardia bidwelli can infect all green parts of the vine. Symptoms: Infected leaves develop reddish-brown circular spots on the upper leaf surface. As lesions mature, the center turns brown and small black pimple-like pycnidia appear. Infected berries become dark brown, covered with black pycnidia, and eventually shrivel into hard black mummies. Most serious fruit infections occur when the grape is pea-sized or larger. Disease cycle: The fungus overwinters in mummified fruit on the vineyard floor or in old fruit clusters hanging in the vines. Spores are released during spring rains to infect new leaves and shoots before bloom. Leaf and shoot infection symptoms appear 10-14 days after a rain period. Fruit infections occur from beginning of bloom until 5-8 weeks later. Native varieties (Vitis labrusca) susceptible until 5-6 weeks after bloom; non-native Vitis vinifera susceptible up to 7-8 weeks after bloom. The period of highest susceptibility for all varieties is from bloom to 4 weeks after bloom. Infection requires susceptible tissue to remain wet for a sufficient time depending on temperature. Disease management: Remove infected prunings and mummified berries before new growth begins. Apply fungicide every 10-14 days from 3-5 inch shoot length through 4-6 weeks after bloom. Less sensitive varieties include Cayuga, Elvira, Verona, and Vidal.

sections.heading: Black Rot overview
sections.text: Black rot is one of the most serious diseases of grapes in the eastern United States. Crop losses can range from 5 to 80 percent depending on the amount of disease in the vineyard, the weather, and variety susceptibility. The fungus Guignardia bidwelli can infect all green parts of the vine. Most damaging is the effect on fruit. Later fruit infections can destroy many grapes, even the entire crop.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.text: Infected leaves develop reddish-brown circular lesions on the upper leaf surface. As lesions mature, the center becomes brown and small black pimple-like fruiting bodies called pycnidia appear, usually arranged in a loose ring just inside a dark border. Infected berries become dark brown and are covered with numerous black pycnidia on the surface. Berries eventually shrivel into hard black mummies. Most serious fruit infections occur when the grape is pea-sized or larger.

sections.heading: Disease Cycle
sections.text: The black rot fungus overwinters in mummified fruit on the vineyard floor or in old fruit clusters hanging in the vines. Infected mummified fruit are the most potent source of spores and eliminating them before new spring growth is critical. The fungus can also overwinter in cane lesions. Spores are released during spring rains to infect new leaves and shoots before bloom. Leaf and shoot infection symptoms appear 10-14 days after a rain period. Fruit infections can occur from the beginning of bloom until 5-8 weeks later. Native varieties (Vitis labrusca such as Concord and Niagara) are susceptible until 5-6 weeks after bloom; non-native Vitis vinifera (like Chardonnay) remain susceptible up to 7-8 weeks after bloom. The period of highest susceptibility for all varieties is bloom to 4 weeks after bloom. Infection occurs when susceptible tissue remains wet long enough at a given temperature.

sections.heading: Infection period by temperature
sections.text: At 50°F: 24 hours of leaf wetness required. At 55°F: 12 hours. At 60°F: 9 hours. At 65°F: 8 hours. At 70°F: 7 hours. At 75°F: 7 hours. At 80°F: 6 hours. At 85°F: 9 hours. At 90°F: 12 hours. Warm, continuous or frequent wetness during bloom and early fruit development is ideal for black rot epidemic development and severe crop loss.

sections.heading: Disease Management
sections.text: Remove, burn and/or bury infected prunings and mummified berries before new spring growth. In vineyards with susceptible varieties or where black rot was a problem the previous year, apply early season fungicide sprays. Apply fungicide every 10-14 days commencing when shoots are 3-5 inches in length, continuing through 4-6 weeks after bloom. During long rainy periods, shorten the interval to 7-10 days. Planting less sensitive varieties such as Cayuga, Elvira, Verona, and Vidal can reduce problems. If already 5-6 weeks past bloom, fungicides will not help much; focus on removing infected material to reduce disease pressure the following year.

sections.heading: Fungicide efficacy (1=very effective, 5=not effective)
sections.text: Mycobutanil: most effective for black rot (rating 1). Maneb and Mancozeb: effective (rating 2). Captan and Copper: moderately effective (rating 4). Sulfur: not effective for black rot (rating 5). Note: sulfur and copper can cause phytotoxicity on sensitive varieties; red/purple varieties (e.g. Concord) are more sensitive to sulfur than white varieties.

sections.heading: Standard spray schedule
sections.text: New shoot (4 inches and again at 10 inches): Captan plus Mycobutanil or Mancozeb plus Mycobutanil — targets Phomopsis and black rot. Before bloom: Captan plus Mycobutanil or Mancozeb plus Mycobutanil — targets black rot and downy mildew. Post-bloom (immediately after bloom): Mancozeb plus Mycobutanil — targets black rot, downy and powdery mildew, botrytis rot. First cover (10 days after post-bloom): Captan plus Sulfur — targets black rot, downy and powdery mildew, botrytis rot. Second cover (2 weeks after first cover): Captan plus Sulfur — targets black rot, downy and powdery mildew, fruit rots. Third and fourth cover (late July or early August): Captan plus Sulfur — targets fruit rots and powdery mildew.

key_phrases: bloom to 4 weeks after bloom highest susceptibility
Vitis labrusca susceptible 5-6 weeks after bloom
Vitis vinifera susceptible 7-8 weeks after bloom
resistant varieties Cayuga Elvira Verona Vidal

content.summary: Esca, Young Esca, and Petri Disease are serious fungal trunk diseases of grapevines caused by a complex of multiple fungi, primarily Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and Phaeoacremonium species. Esca affects older vines while Petri disease/young esca affects young vines. Both diseases are exacerbated by plant stress, especially water stress, and are introduced mainly through unprotected pruning wounds and infected nursery stock. Management relies heavily on preventive cultural practices in nurseries and vineyards, with limited chemical options and some biological control using Trichoderma species.
content.full_text: Grape (Vitis spp.)-Esca, Young Esca, and Petri Disease. Pacific Northwest Plant Disease Management Handbook. Latest revision: March 2025. Cause: Many fungi have been associated with these diseases, although the cause of esca continues to be disputed. Esca (black measles) is a disease of older vines. Initial infection with Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and/or Phaeoacremonium species (Phaeoacremonium minimum most prevalent) is followed by colonization by various basidiomycete fungi (up to 6 genera). Yield losses for individual vines can reach up to 50%, but yield losses are low in vineyards with only esca. Grape quality from affected vines is similar to healthy vines. Young vine decline (Petri disease, young esca) involves Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, 29 Phaeoacremonium species, Pleurostoma richardsiae, and 6 Cadophora species; most prevalent are Phaeoacremonium minimum and Cadophora luteo-olivacea. These fungi are endophytic but become severe pathogens under stress and poor cultural practices. Distributed widely in California and Oregon vineyards aged 10-80 years old. In British Columbia, 7 fungal species have been associated with esca and Petri diseases. Vines are predisposed by stress, especially water stress. Blocked xylem vessels aggravate water stress, leading to insufficient water and nutrient supply. Symptom severity on leaves is directly related to symptom severity in the trunk. Other predisposing factors: malnutrition, poor drainage, soil compaction, heavy crop loads on young plants, poor soil preparation, improper planting holes, and fungal toxins translocated to leaves and berries. Vines from infected mother plants are a significant source of young esca. Pruning wounds remain susceptible for up to 4 months in California, with most infection in the first two months. Early dormant pruning wounds are much more susceptible than late dormant wounds. Poor cold storage and transport of nursery stock can cause stress and cross-contamination. Other associated problems: J-rooted vines, root girdling, graft failure, crown gall, gopher damage, early fruit load, and water stress. Symptoms: Vine decline develops slowly in the first few seasons. Young vines appear normal at planting but show reduced trunk caliper, shortened internodes, reduced foliage and leaf size. In years 1-5, foliar symptoms include interveinal chlorosis, necrosis, early defoliation, wilting, and shoot tip dieback. Uneven wood maturity and rapid desiccation are common. In older vines, leaves show characteristic tiger stripe pattern of severe interveinal chlorosis and necrosis. Apoplexy (sudden vine collapse) occurs mid-season in high-temperature areas. Berries may show purple or brown spotting. Cross-section of trunk shows dark brown to black streaking due to xylem vessel plugging with amber to black gum (gummosis) and tylosis formation. Cultural control: Management starts with preventive practices in nursery mother blocks and propagation beds. Nursery measures: strict sanitation of pruning shears, hydration tanks, grafting machines, and callusing rooms; grow mother vines in well-drained soil with drip irrigation; dip grafted vines in fungicide-impregnated waxes; soak dormant cuttings 30 min in hot water at 122°F/50°C. Vineyard measures: reject poor quality or J-rooted vines at planting; delay fruiting several years until vines have balanced root and shoot growth; avoid grow tubes; plant in spring or fall when water is not limiting; irrigate new plantings for a few years; avoid large pruning cuts during or before wet weather; prune later in the dormant period; use double pruning/prepruning in wet weather; apply pruning wound sealants (B-Lock, Spur Shield, Vitiseal) within 24 hours of cuts; trunk renewal was effective for esca in California. Chemical control: Traditional surface pathogen control techniques do not penetrate dormant cuttings sufficiently to control vascular fungi. Fungicide soaking of cuttings has reduced incidence but none are registered for this use. Biological control: Trichoderma spp. help protect pruning wounds and graft unions. Apply within 24 hours of pruning and again in 2 weeks. Bio-Tam 2.0 (Trichoderma asperellum and T. gamsii) at 1 lb/A in 25-50 gal water, 4-hr reentry, OMRI listed. Vintec (Trichoderma atroviride strain SC1) at 2.8 oz/A in 10-20 gal water, best above 50°F, 4-hr reentry, OMRI listed.

sections.heading: Cause
sections.text: Many fungi are associated with these diseases; the cause of esca remains disputed. Esca (black measles) affects older vines and involves initial infection by Phaeomoniella chlamydospora and/or Phaeoacremonium species (most prevalently Phaeoacremonium minimum), followed by colonization by basidiomycete fungi of up to 6 genera. Yield losses per vine can reach 50% but grape quality is not significantly reduced. Young vine decline (also called Petri disease or young esca) involves Phaeomoniella chlamydospora, 29 Phaeoacremonium species, Pleurostoma richardsiae, and 6 Cadophora species, with Phaeoacremonium minimum and Cadophora luteo-olivacea most prevalent. These fungi are endophytic but become severe pathogens under stress. They are widely distributed in California and Oregon vineyards aged 10-80 years, and 7 associated species have been found in British Columbia surveys.

sections.heading: Predisposing Factors
sections.text: Vines are predisposed to these pathogens by stress, especially water stress. Blocked xylem vessels aggravate water stress and restrict water and nutrient supply. Symptom severity on leaves correlates directly with symptom severity in the trunk. Additional risk factors include malnutrition, poor drainage, soil compaction, heavy crop loads on young vines, poorly prepared soil, improper planting holes, and translocation of fungal toxins to leaves and berries. Infected mother plants are a significant source of young esca inoculum. Pruning wounds remain susceptible to colonization for up to 4 months in California, with most infection in the first two months; early dormant pruning wounds are far more susceptible than late dormant ones. Poor nursery storage and transport conditions can increase stress and enable cross-contamination.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.text: Vine decline develops slowly in the first few seasons after establishment. Young vines appear normal at planting but develop reduced trunk caliper, shortened internodes, reduced foliage, and smaller leaves. In years 1-5, foliar symptoms may include interveinal chlorosis, necrosis, and early defoliation; wilting and shoot tip dieback may also occur. Uneven wood maturity with rapid desiccation is common. In older vines, leaves show a characteristic 'tiger stripe' pattern of severe interveinal chlorosis and necrosis. Apoplexy (sudden symptom appearance and vine collapse) can occur mid-season in high-temperature regions. Berries may develop purple or brown skin spotting. Cross-sections of declining trunks reveal dark brown to black streaking in vascular tissue due to xylem vessel plugging with amber to black gum (gummosis) and tylosis formation.

sections.heading: Cultural Control
sections.text: In the nursery: maintain strict sanitation of pruning shears, hydration tanks, grafting machines, and callusing rooms; grow mother vines in well-drained soil or raised beds using drip irrigation; immediately after grafting, dip vines in fungicide-impregnated waxes containing plant growth regulators; soak dormant cuttings 30 minutes in hot water at 122°F (50°C), combined with other methods. In the vineyard: reject poor quality, J-rooted, or crown-gall-affected vines at planting; delay fruiting several years until vines have balanced root and shoot growth; avoid grow tubes (favors shoot over root growth); plant in spring or fall when water is available and irrigate new plantings for several years; avoid large pruning cuts and pruning during or before wet weather; when large cuts are unavoidable during wet weather, use double pruning (leave a stub to cut later during dry weather); apply wound sealants (B-Lock, Spur Shield, or Vitiseal) within 24 hours of pruning; trunk renewal was effective for esca management in California.

sections.heading: Chemical Control
sections.text: Traditional fungicide techniques for surface pathogens do not penetrate dormant grapevine cuttings adequately to control vascular fungi. Soaking dormant cuttings or vines in fungicides before grafting or planting has reduced disease incidence and severity, but no products are currently registered for this use.

sections.heading: Biological Control
sections.text: Trichoderma species help protect pruning wounds, basal ends of propagation material, and graft unions when applied before infection. Apply within 24 hours of pruning and again 2 weeks later. Registered products: Bio-Tam 2.0 (Trichoderma asperellum and T. gamsii) at 1 lb/A in 25-50 gal water with a dye, 4-hr reentry, OMRI listed; Vintec (Trichoderma atroviride strain SC1) at 2.8 oz/A in 10-20 gal water, most effective above 50°F, 4-hr reentry, OMRI listed.

content.summary: Citrus greening, also called Huanglongbing (HLB), is a bacterial disease of citrus plants considered one of the most serious citrus diseases in the world. There is no cure; infected trees eventually die, usually within a few years. In the United States it is spread by the Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri). The disease has devastated millions of acres of citrus in the U.S. and abroad. Management relies primarily on preventing psyllid introduction, quarantine enforcement, and intensive chemical control of the insect vector. USDA APHIS leads a multi-agency coordination effort to address the disease.
content.full_text: Citrus Greening and Asian Citrus Psyllid. USDA APHIS. Last Modified: January 13, 2026. Citrus greening, also called Huanglongbing (HLB), is a bacterial infection of citrus plants. It is one of the most serious citrus diseases in the world. Once infected, most trees die within a few years. There is no cure. While the disease poses no health threat to people or animals, it has devastated millions of acres of citrus crops throughout the United States and abroad. In the United States, citrus greening is spread by a tiny insect called the Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri Kuwayama). Infected trees produce fruits that are poorly colored, possibly lopsided, and bitter, suitable only for juicing. Citrus greening is currently found throughout Georgia, Florida, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. It is also found in portions of Alabama, California, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Texas. Asian citrus psyllid identification: Eggs are yellow-orange and almond-shaped, often tucked inside crevices and leaf folds. Nymphs are difficult to see but leave behind waxy, white excretions. Adults are gnat-sized, about 1/8-inch long, and jump or fly when approached. Adults have three abdominal colors (blue-green, gray-brown, or orange-yellow), mottled brown wings, and black last two antennal segments. Citrus greening symptoms: Infected trees can remain asymptomatic while serving as a source of bacteria for other trees. Over time, trees produce fewer fruits that are partially green, smaller, irregularly shaped, and bitter. Leaves may show asymmetrical blotchy mottling. Trees may show twig dieback and premature fruit drop. Prevention: Know quarantine boundaries; buy only USDA-certified citrus plants; do not move regulated materials (branches, green waste, dead trees) out of quarantined areas; do not mail or transport home-grown citrus out of quarantined areas; commercial nurseries must follow strict protocols for interstate movement of citrus nursery stock. Treatment: There is no treatment or cure. Infected trees eventually die. The best prevention is preventing introduction of the Asian citrus psyllid. Currently intensive chemical control is the primary management tool to reduce psyllid populations, but this strategy is costly and increasingly ineffective. Multi-Agency Coordination: In 2013, USDA implemented a unified emergency response framework. APHIS leads the group, which includes USDA's Agricultural Research Service (ARS), National Institute of Food and Agriculture (NIFA), U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, state departments of agriculture in Arizona, California, Florida, and Texas, and citrus industry organizations. Potentially Actionable Suspect Sample (PASS) Policy: A PASS is a regulatory sample from the environment or an APHIS-approved exclusionary facility where preliminary diagnostics indicate Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus is present. Samples from non-regulated areas testing positive at a NPPLAP-accredited lab must be forwarded to APHIS' Plant Pathogen Confirmatory Diagnostics Laboratory (PPCDL) in Laurel, MD. Samples from regulated areas need PPCDL confirmation only if detection is unusual (novel symptoms, new host) or would expand a regulated area.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Citrus greening (Huanglongbing/HLB) is a bacterial infection of citrus plants and one of the most serious citrus diseases in the world. There is no cure. Once infected, most trees die within a few years. It poses no health threat to people or animals, but has devastated millions of acres of citrus crops in the United States and abroad. In the United States, the disease is spread by the Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri Kuwayama). Infected trees produce poorly colored, lopsided, and bitter fruit suitable only for juicing.

sections.heading: Current Distribution in the United States
sections.text: Citrus greening is currently found throughout Georgia, Florida, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands. It is also found in portions of Alabama, California, Louisiana, South Carolina, and Texas.

sections.heading: Asian Citrus Psyllid Identification
sections.text: Eggs are yellow-orange and almond-shaped, often found in crevices and leaf folds. Nymphs are difficult to see but leave waxy white excretions on plants. Adults are gnat-sized (about 1/8-inch long) and jump or fly when approached. Adults display three possible abdominal color combinations (blue-green, gray-brown, or orange-yellow), have mottled brown wings, and their last two antennal segments are black.

sections.heading: Citrus Greening Symptoms
sections.text: Once infected, a tree can remain asymptomatic while still serving as a bacterial source for nearby trees. Over time, infected trees produce fewer fruit that are partially green, smaller, irregularly shaped, and bitter. Leaves may show asymmetrical blotchy mottling. Trees may also exhibit twig dieback and premature fruit drop.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.text: Know the quarantine boundaries in your area. Buy only USDA-certified citrus plants. Do not move branches, green waste, dead trees, or other regulated items out of a quarantined area. Do not mail or transport home-grown citrus fruit or plants out of a quarantined area. Commercial nurseries must follow strict protocols for moving citrus nursery stock interstate out of quarantined areas.

sections.heading: Treatment and Management
sections.text: There is no treatment or cure for citrus greening. Infected trees eventually die. The best management strategy is preventing introduction of the Asian citrus psyllid. Currently, intensive chemical control is the primary tool to reduce psyllid populations, but this approach is costly and increasingly ineffective. The scientific community is actively searching for solutions with USDA support.

sections.heading: Huanglongbing Multi-Agency Coordination
sections.text: In 2013, USDA implemented a unified emergency response framework led by APHIS. Partners include USDA's Agricultural Research Service (ARS) and National Institute of Food and Agriculture (NIFA), the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, state departments of agriculture in Arizona, California, Florida, and Texas, and citrus industry organizations in those states. The group coordinates and prioritizes federally funded research and bridges the gap between research and implementation.

sections.heading: Potentially Actionable Suspect Sample (PASS) Policy
sections.text: A PASS is a regulatory sample from the environment or an APHIS-approved exclusionary facility where preliminary diagnostics indicate the presence of Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, a USDA-regulated pathogen. Samples from non-regulated areas testing positive at an NPPLAP-accredited lab must be forwarded to APHIS' Plant Pathogen Confirmatory Diagnostics Laboratory (PPCDL) in Laurel, MD. Samples from regulated areas require PPCDL confirmation only if the detection is unusual (novel symptoms or new host) or would result in expansion of a previously established regulated area. A NPPLAP-certified analyst at an accredited lab may make final determinations on routine samples from regulated areas, provided the APHIS program manager is notified within 24 hours. Sample minimum: 12 symptomatic leaves per sample, from one plant.

sections.heading: Quarantine and Regulatory Framework
sections.text: Federal regulations: 7 CFR 301.76. APHIS publishes an interactive Citrus Federal Quarantine Map and written quarantine descriptions. Regulated articles (plant material, nursery stock, etc.) are listed in a published APHIS document. Stakeholder notifications are issued for quarantine boundary changes for both citrus greening (HLB) and Asian citrus psyllid (ACP).

content.summary: Bacterial spot of peaches is an economically important disease caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. The disease affects leaves, twigs, and fruit and can lead to defoliation, reduced fruit size, and decreased marketability. Management relies on preventive sprays, resistant varieties, and cultural practices that reduce moisture and plant stress.
content.full_text: Bacterial spot is caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni and is a major disease of peaches and other stone fruits. The disease can infect leaves, twigs, and fruit and may cause significant defoliation of trees. Early-season defoliation can reduce fruit size and make fruit more susceptible to sunburn and other quality problems. Symptoms first appear on leaves as small water-soaked lesions that enlarge and turn reddish brown or black. Lesions are often angular because their growth is restricted by leaf veins. As the disease progresses, infected tissue may fall out leaving leaves with a ragged appearance. Fruit infections begin as small water-soaked spots that later form pits or crater-like lesions as surrounding tissue continues to grow. Severe infections reduce fruit quality and profitability in commercial orchards. The bacteria survive in cankers on twigs formed during the previous season and spread during spring by rain splash and insects. High humidity, frequent rainfall, and temperatures above about 75°F favor disease development. Management focuses on preventive sprays, resistant varieties, pruning for airflow, and maintaining healthy trees. Copper-based sprays are commonly used from the dormant period through early fruit development to reduce bacterial populations and disease spread. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

sections.heading: Quick facts
sections.text: Bacterial spot is a major disease of peaches and other stone fruits. It is caused by the bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni. The disease affects leaves, twigs, and fruit and can lead to defoliation and reduced fruit quality. Preventive management strategies are essential because sprays cannot cure existing infections.

sections.heading: Symptoms of bacterial spot
sections.text: Symptoms begin on leaves as small water-soaked lesions that expand and turn reddish brown or black. Lesions are angular because they are restricted by veins. As lesions age, infected tissue may fall out leaving a ragged leaf appearance. Fruit infections begin as small water-soaked spots that develop into pits or crater-like lesions as the fruit grows.

sections.heading: Disease cycle
sections.text: The bacteria overwinter in cankers on twigs formed during the previous season. In spring the bacteria ooze from these sites and are spread by rain splash and insects to developing leaves and fruit. Warm temperatures and high humidity promote disease spread.

sections.heading: Conditions favoring disease
sections.text: High moisture conditions such as frequent rainfall, heavy dew, and high humidity favor infection. Temperatures above approximately 75°F increase bacterial spread and disease development.

sections.heading: Disease management
sections.text: Management relies on preventive practices including pruning to improve air movement, planting less susceptible varieties, fertilizing trees properly to maintain vigor, and applying copper sprays during dormant and early growing periods. Cultural practices that reduce leaf wetness and plant stress help limit disease severity.

content.summary: Bacterial leaf spot, caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria, is the most common and destructive disease of peppers in the eastern United States. It is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium that survives in seeds and plant debris. The disease causes water-soaked leaf lesions, fruit spots, stem cankers, premature defoliation, and yield loss. Different strains of the pathogen affect different cultivars due to host specificity. Management relies on an integrated strategy combining resistant varieties, certified disease-free seeds, seed treatments, copper-based foliar sprays combined with plant resistance inducers, crop rotation, and sanitation.
content.full_text: Bacterial Leaf Spot of Pepper. WVU Extension, West Virginia University. Bacterial leaf spot, caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria, is the most common and destructive disease for peppers in the eastern United States. It is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium that can survive in seeds and plant debris from one season to another. Different strains or races of the bacterium are cultivar-specific, causing disease symptoms in certain varieties due to stringent host specificity. Bacterial leaf spot can devastate a pepper crop by early defoliation of infected leaves and disfiguring fruit. In severe cases, plants may die. Symptoms: Disease symptoms can appear throughout the above-ground portion of the plant, including leaf spot, fruit spot, and stem canker. Early symptoms show up as water-soaked lesions on leaves that can quickly change from green to dark brown and enlarge into spots up to 1/4 inch in diameter with slightly raised margins. In less humid weather, spots dry up and damaged tissues fall off, resulting in a tattered appearance. Lesion size is variable with irregular and angular margins. On some cultivars, leaves display several small lesions (1/4 to 1/2 cm) covering over 80% of leaf area; on others, fewer large lesions (larger than 1/2 cm) are visible. Yield is drastically reduced as affected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely, exposing fruit to sunscald. Raised, scab-like spots on fruit render fruit unmarketable. Lesions on fruit can allow entry of other pathogens, causing fruit decay. Disease cycle and epidemiology: Bacterial spot develops most rapidly during periods of warm temperatures and prolonged wet conditions. The pathogen survives in and on seeds and in plant debris, and can survive in debris for at least a year. Once exposed to soil, it survives only a few weeks. Infected weeds and volunteer plants are also inoculum sources. Disease development is favored by relative humidity above 85%, extended leaf wetness, and heat waves with night temperatures above 70°F. Short periods (3 or more days) below 40% relative humidity reduce disease severity. Extended periods of at least 3 weeks of low humidity irreversibly halt disease spread even if favorable conditions return. In colder climates, infection is mostly from contaminated seeds. Infected dried seeds stored cold for 10 years can still produce diseased plants. Bacteria spread within fields via wind-driven rain, irrigation droplets, aerosols, and handling of wet plants. Disease management: Management follows the DM=S2IR formula: stop or reduce (1) survival, (2) spread, (3) infection, and (4) reproduction of the pathogen. Methods include: using resistant varieties, seed treatment, foliage treatment, and integrated strategy. Resistant varieties: Up to 11 strains of the pathogen are known; resistant varieties must resist all prevalent strains. Sweet pepper varieties with multi-strain resistance include Autry, Green Flash, Labelle, Ninja, Outsider, Playmaker, Prowler, Raven, Samurai, SB3255 PB, and Tracer. Green Machine, Antebellum (X10R) and 4288a (bs5+bs6 genes) have intermediate resistance. Use pathogen-free certified seeds and disease-free transplants. Grow own transplants under sanitary conditions. Seed treatments: Wash seeds 40 minutes in diluted Clorox (2 parts Clorox + 8 parts water) to reduce surface bacteria, though internal bacteria are little affected. Hot water treatment (30 minutes at 125°F/51°C) reduces bacteria on surface and inside seeds but may affect germination if not done accurately. Foliage treatment: Inspect and remove symptomatic transplants. Early-stage transplants may be treated with streptomycin, though resistance to streptomycin may arise with multiple applications. Copper sprays are more effective when combined with plant resistance inducers such as Regalia (organic) or Actigard. Actigard rates: 1 oz/100 gal at transplant, 0.5 oz two weeks later, then 1/3 oz for subsequent sprays. Apply on a 7-10-day schedule; shorten interval during rain, high humidity, warm temperatures. Penncozeb and Quintec showed effectiveness in some studies. Biological products Serenade and Sonata can reduce leaf spot if used proactively, but are ineffective once disease exceeds 5% of plants. Control solanaceous weeds (nightshade, horsenettle, jimsonweed). Disk or plow field immediately after harvest. Use at least a 3-year crop rotation; avoid rotating pepper with tomato, eggplant, or potato. Replace overhead irrigation with drip irrigation. In greenhouse: discard trays adjacent to outbreaks; sanitize all supplies between crops. Integrated management: No single measure is adequate; an integrated strategy incorporating all the above methods, plus field scouting, removing infected plants, and timing operations to minimize spread, is most effective.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.text: Bacterial leaf spot, caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. vesicatoria, is the most common and destructive disease for peppers in the eastern United States. It is a gram-negative, rod-shaped bacterium that survives in seeds and plant debris from season to season. Different strains or races of the bacterium are cultivar-specific due to stringent host specificity. The disease can devastate a pepper crop through early defoliation and fruit disfigurement. In severe cases, plants may die. However, several preventive and management options are available.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.text: Symptoms appear throughout the above-ground plant, including leaf spots, fruit spots, and stem cankers. Early symptoms are water-soaked lesions on leaves that rapidly turn from green to dark brown and enlarge to up to 1/4 inch in diameter with slightly raised margins. In less humid conditions, spots dry and necrotic tissue falls out, creating a tattered appearance. Lesion size is variable with irregular, angular margins. Some cultivars show numerous small lesions (1/4 to 1/2 cm) covering over 80% of the leaf; others show fewer large lesions (over 1/2 cm). Affected leaves turn yellow and drop prematurely, reducing productivity and exposing fruit to sunscald. Raised, scab-like fruit spots render fruit unmarketable, and lesions can allow secondary fruit-rotting pathogens to enter.

sections.heading: Disease Cycle and Epidemiology
sections.text: Bacterial spot develops most rapidly during warm temperatures and prolonged wet conditions. The pathogen survives in and on seeds and in plant debris for at least one year; once debris decomposes and the pathogen contacts soil, it survives only a few weeks. Infected weeds and volunteer plants are also inoculum sources. Conditions favorable for disease development include relative humidity above 85%, extended leaf wetness, and heat waves with night temperatures above 70°F. Three or more days below 40% relative humidity reduce disease severity. At least three weeks of low humidity irreversibly halts disease spread, even if favorable conditions return afterward. In colder climates, seed-borne infection is primary; even seeds stored cold for 10 years can produce diseased plants. Within fields, bacteria spread by wind-driven rain, irrigation droplets, aerosols, and handling of wet plants.

sections.heading: Disease Management — Using Resistant Varieties
sections.text: Up to 11 strains of the pathogen are known; resistant varieties must resist all prevalent strains in the growing area. Sweet pepper varieties with resistance to all or multiple strains include Autry, Green Flash, Labelle, Ninja, Outsider, Playmaker, Prowler, Raven, Samurai, SB3255 PB, and Tracer. Green Machine, Antebellum (X10R), and 4288a (bs5+bs6 genes) provide intermediate resistance. The most effective strategy is using pathogen-free certified seeds and disease-free transplants. Grow own transplants under sanitary conditions; inspect carefully and reject infected transplants.

sections.heading: Disease Management — Seed Treatments
sections.text: Surface disinfection: wash seeds 40 minutes in diluted Clorox (2 parts Clorox plus 8 parts water). This reduces bacteria on the seed surface but does not affect internal bacteria. Hot water treatment: soak seeds 30 minutes in water pre-heated to 125°F (51°C). This reduces bacteria on the surface and inside seeds, but can reduce germination if not done accurately; risk is relatively low with bleach treatment.

sections.heading: Disease Management — Foliage Treatment
sections.text: Regularly inspect greenhouse transplants; remove and destroy symptomatic plants or treat with streptomycin at early disease stages (note: repeated streptomycin use can lead to resistant strains). Copper sprays combined with plant resistance inducers provide better protection than copper alone. Actigard (conventional) or Regalia (organic) are recommended inducers. Actigard rates: 1 oz/100 gal at transplant, 0.5 oz two weeks later, then 1/3 oz for subsequent applications. Apply on a 7-10-day schedule, shortening to 7 days during rainy, humid, warm periods. Penncozeb and Quintec also showed effectiveness in some studies. Biological products Serenade and Sonata are proactively effective but cannot suppress disease once it exceeds 5% of plants. Control solanaceous weeds (nightshade, horsenettle, jimsonweed) in and around fields. Disk or plow field immediately after final harvest to hasten residue decomposition. Use at least a 3-year crop rotation, avoiding sequences of pepper with tomato, eggplant, or potato. Switch from overhead to drip irrigation; do not access fields when plants are wet. In greenhouses: discard trays adjacent to outbreaks; sanitize trays, benches, tools, and greenhouse structures between crops.

sections.heading: Disease Management — Integrated Strategy
sections.text: The best management is achieved through an integrated strategy, as a single measure such as seed treatment alone is insufficient. The integrated approach combines resistant varieties, certified seeds, seed treatments, foliage treatments, crop rotation, sanitation, field scouting to time treatments, removal of infected plants, and scheduling mechanical/labor operations to minimize disease spread risk. The management formula DM=S2IR represents: disease management = stopping or reducing (1) survival, (2) spread, (3) infection, and (4) reproduction of the pathogen.

content.summary: Early blight is one of the most common tomato and potato diseases in Minnesota, occurring nearly every season. It is caused by two closely related fungal species — Alternaria tomatophila and Alternaria solani — and affects leaves, stems, and fruit. The disease can cause severe defoliation and is yield-limiting when susceptible cultivars are used under favorable weather conditions. Management relies on resistant varieties, cultural practices to reduce leaf wetness and soil splash, sanitation, and timely fungicide applications on farms with rotation of chemical families to prevent resistance.
content.full_text: Early Blight in Tomato and Potato. UMN Extension, University of Minnesota. Reviewed 2024. Early blight is one of the most common tomato and potato diseases, occurring nearly every season in Minnesota. It affects leaves, fruits, and stems and can be severely yield-limiting when susceptible tomato cultivars are used and weather is favorable. Severe defoliation can occur. In tomatoes, fruit can be damaged by sun when defoliation exposes them. Identification: Initially, small dark spots form on older foliage near the ground. Leaf spots are round, brown, and can grow up to 1/2 inch in diameter. Larger spots have target-like concentric rings; tissue around spots often turns yellow. Severely infected leaves turn brown and fall off, or dead, dried leaves cling to the stem. Seedling stems are infected at or just above the soil line; the stem turns brown, sunken, and dry (collar rot). If the infection girdles the stem, the seedling wilts and dies. Stem infections on older plants are oval to irregular, dry brown areas with dark brown concentric rings. Fruit can be infected at any maturity stage; fruit spots are leathery and black with raised concentric ridges, generally near the stem. Infected fruit may drop from the plant. Biology: Early blight can be caused by two closely related species: Alternaria tomatophila and Alternaria solani. Both pathogens can infect tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, and several solanaceous weeds including black nightshade (Solanum ptycanthum) and hairy nightshade (Solanum physalifolium). Disease develops at moderate to warm temperatures (59-80°F); optimum is 82-86°F. The pathogen is most likely to spread with wet weather, heavy dew, or relative humidity of 90% or greater. Both pathogens overwinter in infected plant debris and soil; the pathogen also survives on tomato seed or may be introduced on transplants. Lower leaves become infected through direct contact with contaminated soil or when raindrops splash soil onto leaves. Spores can germinate between 47° and 90°F and need free water or relative humidity of 90% or greater. Spores infect plants and form leaf spots as small as 1/8 inch in as little as five days. Spores spread throughout a field by wind, human contact, or equipment, resulting in many reinfection opportunities. Home garden management: Resistant varieties are readily available and recommended. Resistance does not mean immunity; damage will be less severe. Cultural controls: cover soil under plants with mulch (fabric, straw, plastic, or dried leaves); water at the base using drip irrigation or soaker hose; increase airflow by staking, trellising, and removing weeds; prune bottom leaves to prevent soil splash; rotate away from tomatoes or peppers for at least two years. Physical controls: remove leaves with spots and bury or burn them; wash hands and sanitize tools after touching infected plants; remove up to one-third of leaves if caught early; keep leaves dry. Most home gardeners do not need to treat with fungicide. Farm management: Scout regularly starting in mid to late June. Use pathogen-free seed or collect seed from disease-free plants. Rotate out of tomatoes and related crops for at least two years. Control solanaceous weeds and volunteer tomatoes throughout the rotation. Fertilize properly; maintain adequate nitrogen and phosphorus, do not over-fertilize with potassium. Avoid working in wet plants; use drip irrigation. Stake, trellis, and prune plants for airflow. Apply plastic or organic mulch. Remove or bury infected plants in fall. In greenhouse production, covering with UV-absorbing vinyl film has reduced early blight by up to 50%. For fungicides: apply when conditions favor disease; alternate between chemical families to avoid resistance. Some insensitivity to chemical family 11 (FRAC Group 11) has become more common; rotate with other chemical families. Consult the Midwest Vegetable Production Guide for current recommendations.

sections.heading: Quick Facts
sections.text: Early blight is one of the most common tomato and potato diseases, occurring nearly every season in Minnesota. It affects leaves, fruits, and stems and can be severely yield-limiting when susceptible tomato cultivars are used and weather is favorable. Severe defoliation can occur. In tomatoes, exposed fruit can be damaged by sunscald.

sections.heading: Identification
sections.text: Initially, small dark spots form on older foliage near the ground. Leaf spots are round, brown, and can grow up to 1/2 inch in diameter. Larger spots have target-like concentric rings; tissue around spots often turns yellow. Severely infected leaves turn brown and fall off, or dead dried leaves cling to the stem. Seedling stems are infected at or just above the soil line, turning brown, sunken, and dry (collar rot); if the infection girdles the stem, the seedling wilts and dies. Stem infections on older plants appear as oval to irregular dry brown areas with dark brown concentric rings. Fruit spots are leathery and black with raised concentric ridges, generally near the stem; infected fruit may drop from the plant.

sections.heading: Biology
sections.text: Early blight is caused by two closely related species: Alternaria tomatophila and Alternaria solani. Both pathogens infect tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, and solanaceous weeds including black nightshade (Solanum ptycanthum) and hairy nightshade (Solanum physalifolium). Disease develops at moderate to warm temperatures (59-80°F); the optimum range is 82-86°F. The pathogen spreads most readily during wet weather, heavy dew, or when relative humidity is 90% or greater. Both pathogens overwinter in infected plant debris and soil in Minnesota; the pathogen also survives on tomato seed or may arrive on transplants. Lower leaves become infected when soil splashes onto them or through direct soil contact. Spores germinate between 47° and 90°F with free water or relative humidity of 90% or greater, forming leaf spots as small as 1/8 inch in as little as five days. Spores spread through a field by wind, human contact, and equipment.

sections.heading: Home Garden Management — Cultural and Physical Controls
sections.text: Use resistant varieties (damage will be less severe than with non-resistant varieties; Cornell University tracks varieties with disease resistance). Cultural controls: cover soil under plants with mulch (fabric, straw, plastic, or dried leaves); water at the plant base using drip irrigation or soaker hose; increase airflow by staking, trellising, and removing weeds; prune bottom leaves to reduce soil splash onto foliage; rotate away from tomatoes or peppers for at least two years. Physical controls: remove leaves with spots and bury or burn them; wash hands and sanitize tools after touching infected plants; remove up to one-third of plant leaves if disease is caught early; keep leaves dry. Most home gardeners do not need fungicide treatment.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Monitoring
sections.text: Early blight typically appears in Minnesota in mid to late June. The exact timing varies year to year, so scout regularly to begin management as soon as the disease appears.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Cultural Controls
sections.text: Use pathogen-free seed or collect seed only from disease-free plants. Rotate out of tomatoes and related crops for at least two years. Control solanaceous weeds such as black nightshade and hairy nightshade, and volunteer tomato plants throughout the rotation. Fertilize properly to maintain vigorous growth; maintain adequate nitrogen and phosphorus levels and do not over-fertilize with potassium. Avoid working in plants when wet; use drip rather than overhead irrigation. Stake, trellis, and prune plants to increase airflow and reduce soil-foliage contact. Apply plastic or organic mulch as a barrier between contaminated soil and leaves. Prune and carefully dispose of infected leaves away from production areas; sanitize tools during pruning. Remove or bury infected plants in fall. In greenhouse production, covering with UV-absorbing vinyl film has reduced early blight by up to 50%.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Fungicides
sections.text: Apply fungicides when environmental conditions favor disease and repeat according to label instructions. Alternate between different chemical families to avoid the development of pathogen insensitivity. Some insensitivity to FRAC Group 11 (chemical family 11) has become more common in some areas; rotate Group 11 fungicides with other chemical families, as Group 11 products will not provide good control where insensitivity is already established. Consult the Midwest Vegetable Production Guide (mwveguide.org) for current recommendations.
