content.summary: Late blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans (a water mold, not a true fungus), is a potentially devastating disease of tomato and potato that can result in total crop failure if untreated. It favors cool (60–70°F), damp conditions and spreads rapidly via airborne sporangia. The disease is historically significant as the cause of the Irish potato famine of the late 1840s. It does not occur every year in Minnesota. Management combines monitoring, cultural controls, resistant varieties, and water-mold-specific fungicides applied preventatively.
content.full_text: Late Blight of Tomato and Potato. UMN Extension, University of Minnesota. Reviewed 2021. Late blight is a potentially devastating disease of tomato and potato, infecting leaves, stems, tomato fruit, and potato tubers. The disease spreads quickly in fields and can result in total crop failure if untreated. Late blight does not occur every year in Minnesota. Late blight of potato was responsible for the Irish potato famine of the late 1840s. Symptoms: Leaves have large, dark brown blotches with a green-gray edge, not confined by major leaf veins. Infections progress through leaflets and petioles, resulting in large sections of dry brown foliage. Stem infections are firm and dark brown with a rounded edge. In cool, wet weather, entire fields turn brown and wilted as if hit by frost. In tomatoes, firm, dark brown, circular spots grow to cover large parts of fruits and may become mushy as secondary bacteria invade. In high humidity, thin powdery white fungal growth appears on infected leaves, tomato fruit, and stems. Infected potato tubers become discolored (brown, red, or purple) and are invaded by secondary soft rot bacteria. Biology: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is a water mold. It favors cool (60-70°F), damp conditions; prolonged hot dry days can halt pathogen spread. The most common routes of introduction each season are infected potato seed tubers, infected tomato transplants from other regions, or windblown sporangia from the south. P. infestans can overwinter in Minnesota in potato cull piles; overwintering in tomato systems is unlikely, but infected tomato fruits may give rise to volunteer seedlings the following season. Under cool, wet conditions, P. infestans can infect and produce thousands of sporangia per lesion in less than five days. There are many different strains (clonal lineages, designated by number codes like US-23). Many lineages affect both tomato and potato; some are host-specific. Host range is typically limited to potato and tomato, but hairy nightshade (Solanum physalifolium) can readily become infected. Petunia may also become infected under ideal greenhouse conditions. Home garden management: Scout regularly; remove diseased leaves. Resistant varieties are available but even resistant varieties will show some symptoms under highly favorable conditions. Cultural controls: plant in areas not used for tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, or eggplant for 3-4 years; use drip or soaker hose irrigation; water in the morning; stake or cage plants; space for airflow; remove or bury plants at season end; manage cull piles. Pesticide sprays are not recommended for home gardens. Farm management: Scout early in the day before dew dries, paying extra attention to shaded or poorly aerated areas. Destroy potato cull piles before the growing season by burying, incorporating, or feeding to livestock. Control volunteer potato plants. Plant certified potato seed. Inspect purchased tomato transplants for late blight symptoms. If found in few plants, remove, disc under, kill with herbicide or flame-kill to prevent field spread. Resistant varieties vary in effectiveness by cultivar and by P. infestans clonal lineage present. Fungicide applications should be made prior to infection when conditions favor disease. Because P. infestans is a water mold (not a true fungus), fungicides specific to water molds must be used. Rotate fungicide groups and/or tank mix to avoid resistance. Consult the Midwest Vegetable Production Guide for current fungicide recommendations.

sections.heading: Quick Facts
sections.text: Late blight is a potentially devastating disease of tomato and potato, infecting leaves, stems, tomato fruit, and potato tubers. The disease spreads quickly and can result in total crop failure if untreated. Late blight does not occur every year in Minnesota. Late blight of potato was responsible for the Irish potato famine of the late 1840s.

sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.text: Leaves show large, dark brown blotches with a green-gray edge, not confined by major leaf veins. Infections progress through leaflets and petioles, resulting in large sections of dry brown foliage. Stem infections are firm and dark brown with a rounded edge. In cool, wet weather, entire fields can turn brown and wilted as if hit by frost. In tomatoes, firm, dark brown, circular spots grow to cover large portions of fruit and may become mushy as secondary bacteria invade. In high humidity, thin powdery white growth appears on infected leaves, fruit, and stems. Infected potato tubers become discolored (brown, red, or purple) and are invaded by secondary soft rot bacteria.

sections.heading: Biology
sections.text: Late blight is caused by Phytophthora infestans, a water mold — not a true fungus. It favors cool (60–70°F), damp conditions; prolonged hot, dry days can halt pathogen spread. Common seasonal introduction routes include infected potato seed tubers, infected tomato transplants from other regions, and windblown sporangia (asexual spores) from the south. P. infestans can overwinter in Minnesota in potato cull piles; overwintering in tomato systems is unlikely, but infected tomato fruits may give rise to infected volunteer seedlings the following season. Under cool, wet conditions, P. infestans can infect and produce thousands of sporangia per lesion in fewer than five days, leading to rapid airborne spread. There are many strains (clonal lineages, e.g., US-23); many affect both tomato and potato, while some are host-specific. The host range is typically limited to potato and tomato, though hairy nightshade (Solanum physalifolium) can become infected and may contribute to spread; petunia may also be infected under greenhouse conditions.

sections.heading: Home Garden Management
sections.text: Scout tomatoes regularly and remove diseased leaves. Note that late blight is just one of many tomato issues and is not present every growing season. Resistant varieties are available, though even resistant cultivars may show some symptoms under highly favorable conditions. Cultural controls: plant tomatoes where no tomatoes, potatoes, peppers, or eggplant have grown for the past 3-4 years; use drip or soaker hose irrigation; water in the morning so leaves dry quickly; stake or cage plants; space for airflow; remove or bury plants at season end and manage cull piles. Pesticide sprays are not recommended for home gardens.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Monitoring
sections.text: Scout early in the day before dew dries. Pay extra attention to portions of the field that dry more slowly — areas shaded by trees or with poor air circulation. Detecting late blight early is important because neighboring crops are at risk as a region-wide issue. Report suspected cases to the University of Minnesota Fruit and Vegetable team. Regional outbreak reports are available at USAblight.org.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Cultural Controls
sections.text: Destroy potato cull piles before the growing season by burying, incorporating into fields, or feeding to livestock. Control volunteer potato plants that may grow from infected tubers. Plant certified potato seed. Inspect purchased tomato transplants for late blight symptoms prior to planting, as transplants from southern regions may be infected. If infection is found in only a few plants, remove, disc under, kill with herbicide, or flame-kill to prevent spread throughout the field.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Resistant Varieties
sections.text: Resistant varieties are available but will still show some symptoms under highly favorable conditions. Resistance levels vary by cultivar and may be more or less effective depending on the P. infestans clonal lineage present. Cornell compiles lists of disease-resistant vegetable varieties.

sections.heading: Farm Management — Fungicides
sections.text: Fungicide applications should be made prior to infection when environmental conditions favor disease, to be most effective. Because P. infestans is a water mold — not a true fungus — fungicides specific to water molds must be used; standard fungicides may not be effective. Rotate fungicide groups and/or tank mix to avoid producing fungicide-resistant isolates. Consult the Midwest Vegetable Production Guide (mwveguide.org) for a current list of recommended fungicides for late blight management.
